was a [parliamentary borough](/wiki/Parliamentary_borough "Parliamentary borough") in [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk"), which elected two [Members of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MPs) to the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") from 1558 until it was abolished by the [Great Reform Act 1832](/wiki/Great_Reform_Act_1832 "Great Reform Act 1832"). Its famous members of Parliament included the future [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister "Prime Minister") [Robert Walpole](/wiki/Robert_Walpole,_1st_Earl_of_Orford "Robert Walpole, 1st Earl of Orford") and the diarist [Samuel Pepys](/wiki/Samuel_Pepys "Samuel Pepys").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

UK parliamentary constituency in England, 1558–1832

| Castle Rising | |
| --- | --- |
| Former [borough constituency](/wiki/Borough_constituency "Borough constituency") for the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") | |
| 1558–[1832](/wiki/1832_United_Kingdom_general_election "1832 United Kingdom general election") | |
| Seats | Two |

**Castle Rising** was a [parliamentary borough](/wiki/Parliamentary_borough "Parliamentary borough") in [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk"), which elected two [Members of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MPs) to the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") from 1558 until it was abolished by the [Great Reform Act 1832](/wiki/Great_Reform_Act_1832 "Great Reform Act 1832"). Its famous members of Parliament included the future [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister "Prime Minister") [Robert Walpole](/wiki/Robert_Walpole,_1st_Earl_of_Orford "Robert Walpole, 1st Earl of Orford") and the diarist [Samuel Pepys](/wiki/Samuel_Pepys "Samuel Pepys").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Castle_Rising_(UK_Parliament_constituency)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The borough extended over four parishes - [Castle Rising](/wiki/Castle_Rising "Castle Rising"), [Roydon](/wiki/Roydon,_King%27s_Lynn_and_West_Norfolk "Roydon, King's Lynn and West Norfolk"), [North Wootton](/wiki/North_Wootton,_Norfolk "North Wootton, Norfolk") and [South Wootton](/wiki/South_Wootton "South Wootton"), in rural Norfolk to the north-east of [King's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn "King's Lynn"). Castle Rising had once been a market town and seaport, but long before the passing of the Reform Act 1832 had declined to little more than a village. In 1831, the population of the borough was 888, and contained 169 houses.

Castle Rising was a [burgage](/wiki/Burgage "Burgage") borough, meaning that the right to vote was vested in the owners of particular properties ("burgage tenements"), and that consequently the absolute right to nominate both the MPs could be bought and sold. Although it was possible for the landowner to create multiple voters by giving a reliable nominee notional ownership of the tenements – as was done in many other burgage boroughs – in Castle Rising the number of voters was kept as low as possible, and contested elections were almost unknown.

The [Lord of the Manor](/wiki/Lord_of_the_Manor "Lord of the Manor") invariably owned a majority of the burgage tenements, though other influential local families were generally allowed to select the second MP. In the seventeenth century the [Duke of Norfolk](/wiki/Duke_of_Norfolk "Duke of Norfolk") was the dominant interest: it was the Norfolk interest which enabled Samuel Pepys to gain the seat in 1673. At the start of the 18th century, the borough belonged to the Walpole family, and [Sir Robert Walpole](/wiki/Robert_Walpole "Robert Walpole") (Britain's first Prime Minister) began his parliamentary career here. Later in the century the Walpoles still nominated one MP, and the [Earl of Suffolk](/wiki/Earl_of_Suffolk "Earl of Suffolk") the other.[[1]](#cite_note-1) By 1816 the patronage had passed to the [Earl of Cholmondeley](/wiki/Earl_of_Cholmondeley "Earl of Cholmondeley") and Richard Howard.

Castle Rising was abolished as a constituency by the [Reform Act 1832](/wiki/Reform_Act_1832 "Reform Act 1832").

**Notes**

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** Pages 145 and 146, [Lewis Namier](/wiki/Lewis_Namier "Lewis Namier"), *[The Structure of Politics at the Accession of George III](/wiki/The_Structure_of_Politics_at_the_Accession_of_George_III "The Structure of Politics at the Accession of George III")* (2nd edition - London: St Martin's Press, 1957), The Walpole family are noted as "Earl of Orford".
2. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** ["History of Parliament"](http://www.historyofparliamentonline.org/volume/1509-1558/constituencies/castle-rising). Retrieved 11 October 2011.
3. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-HoP3_3-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-HoP3_3-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-HoP3_3-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-HoP3_3-3) [***e***](#cite_ref-HoP3_3-4) [***f***](#cite_ref-HoP3_3-5) [***g***](#cite_ref-HoP3_3-6) [***h***](#cite_ref-HoP3_3-7) [***i***](#cite_ref-HoP3_3-8) [***j***](#cite_ref-HoP3_3-9) ["History of Parliament"](http://www.historyofparliamentonline.org/volume/1558-1603/constituencies/castle-rising). Retrieved 11 October 2011.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** Hatton was also elected for [Higham Ferrers](/wiki/Higham_Ferrers_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Higham Ferrers (UK Parliament constituency)"), which he chose to represent, and never sat for Castle Rising.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** Walpole was also elected for [King's Lynn](/wiki/King%27s_Lynn_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "King's Lynn (UK Parliament constituency)"), which he chose to represent, and did not sit again for Castle Rising.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** Wedderburn was also elected for [Okehampton](/wiki/Okehampton_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Okehampton (UK Parliament constituency)"), which he chose to represent, and never sat for Castle Rising.
